title: DNS Rebinding to API Key Disclosure and Full Instance Control

cvss_v3_1.notes: AC:H due to DNS rebinding complexity. A:H because attacker can create ReDoS profiles or modify critical settings.

cwe: CWE-350: Reliance on Reverse DNS Resolution for a Security-Critical Action

validation.notes: Validated that no HostFiltering middleware exists and /initialize.json returns API key unauthenticated. DNS rebinding itself not executed in lab -- chain validated component by component.

description:
Radarr has no Host header validation middleware (UseHostFiltering or AllowedHosts configuration). When AuthenticationMethod is None (default on fresh install) or External (reverse proxy auth), the /initialize.json endpoint is accessible without credentials and returns the API key in plaintext.

A DNS rebinding attack from a malicious website can exploit this by first resolving to the attacker's server (to serve the exploit JavaScript), then re-resolving to the victim's Radarr instance IP. Since the browser considers this same-origin, /initialize.json is readable and the API key is extracted. Subsequent API calls use the stolen key.

The External auth mode is equally affected because it uses NoAuthenticationHandler internally -- if the raw Radarr port is reachable on the LAN (bypassing the reverse proxy), all requests are auto-authenticated.

root_cause.detail: No UseHostFiltering() or AllowedHosts configuration in the request pipeline
root_cause.secondary_detail: Line 49: apiKey included in unauthenticated JSON response when auth=None

remediation: Add UseHostFiltering() middleware with AllowedHosts configured to the expected hostname
Require API key authentication for /initialize.json regardless of auth mode
Force authentication setup on first run (do not default to AuthenticationMethod=None)
